AlphaChip case shows why differences in workloads, baselines, budgets, and tool
settings must remain visible. AI can organize related runs and point reviewers
toward conflicting results, but automatic summaries can also erase those
differences. The open problem is whether an AI interface linked to the
underlying artifacts can reduce review time while helping reviewers find more
unsupported claims and important disagreements than current practice.

5. **Can AI help architects decide what to do next without overstating the
evidence?**
An architecture project can remain stuck after evaluation because no one has
connected the evidence to a clear action. AI could organize evidence for and
against a candidate, state unresolved risks, and identify what would reopen the
decision. The question is whether that assistance improves on structured
templates and expert preparation while keeping the recommendation within the
evidence and the final choice with the responsible architect.

6. **Does AI reduce end-to-end engineering work, or merely move it?**
The compounded challenge described in this chapter means that a local speedup
can create more work in evaluation, tool integration, debugging, verification,
or review. Any end-to-end comparison with current architecture practice must
count recommendation quality, engineering time, tool cost, invalid runs, review
effort, time to a decision, and the work required to check the AI system. The
open question is whether the total burden falls or merely moves to a less
visible part of the project.

## Conclusion

Architecture has always relied on feedback, and it has repeatedly adapted by
making interfaces, design rules, tool outputs, and comparison standards
explicit. AI now enters a process that must also coordinate specialization,
heterogeneous integration, data movement, changing software, physical
constraints, and expensive validation. Together, these problems can keep the
work from reaching a defensible decision in several different ways.

AI-assisted architecture must handle five practical challenges.

- **Relevant information must be available and current.** AI cannot account for
  a workload, software assumption, interface, or physical constraint that the
  available inputs omit, and stale assumptions can invalidate an otherwise
  coherent result.
- **The design space must contain valid choices.** Specialization and chiplets
  create many possible designs, but their validity depends on hardware,
  software, packaging, and physical constraints that must be considered
  together.
- **Decisive feedback may remain expensive.** Cheap models and surrogates can
  screen candidates, but omitted physical effects can reverse their rankings,
  while simulation, implementation, and verification consume scarce resources.
- **Architects and reviewers have finite time.** Faster generation helps only
  when weak candidates can be rejected and conflicting evidence can be
  examined before they consume more tool time and expert attention.
- **The evidence must support a defined decision.** A result is useful only when
  the responsible architect can say what action it supports and who is
  responsible for taking that action.

AI assistance is useful only when it helps architects manage one or more of
these challenges without weakening the comparison or moving the burden
elsewhere.
Another architect should be able to inspect the assumptions, tool results,
failed and rejected alternatives, evidence, and final decision, then judge
whether the assistance helped. Producing more candidates does not improve an
architecture project when the comparison remains incomplete, the decisive checks remain
unaffordable, or the next action remains unclear.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Match AI's role to a specific architecture challenge and
  measure whether it reduced that burden.
- **Reader test:** Can you explain which challenge or combination of challenges
  limits a project and what evidence would show that AI reduced the burden rather
  than shifted it elsewhere?
- **Up next:** @sec-architecture-20-ontology shows what architects must make
  explicit about the question, available choices, tool path, evidence, and next
  decision so an AI system and another architect can work on the same problem.
:::
